ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese eel porridge; mapped to the broader 'porridge food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0GXM for rice porridge/congee used as the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for eel-enriched Vietnamese porridge. The eel and herb components are not captured by this code but no closer approved FoodEx2 entry exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese eel porridge; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Nghe An eel porridge is approximately 450 g, as commonly served at Vietnamese street-food stalls and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Use in East Asia — eel and rice porridge components; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for freshwater eel (NDB 15032) and plain rice porridge
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Cháo lươn Nghệ An as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-ingredient modelling: approximately 15% cooked freshwater eel (USDA SR Legacy NDB 15032), 70% plain rice porridge (USDA SR Legacy NDB 20050), and 15% seasoning/herb fraction (fish sauce, turmeric, ginger, shallots, garlic, chilli). Input per_100g values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were reviewed against this model and retained where consistent. Energy cross-checked via Atwater factors: protein 6.67 g × 4 + fat 3.33 g × 9 + carbohydrate 8.44 g × 4 = 26.68 + 29.97 + 33.76 = 90.41 kcal ≈ 93.33 kcal (within acceptable rounding). Values accepted as plausible.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice congee / rice porridge analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database specifically for Vietnamese eel porridge. The GI of 72 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: rice congee/porridge (GI ~72, high category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The eel and herb fraction (~30% of dish by weight) does not substantially alter the GI of the rice-dominant matrix. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 72 × carbohydrate per serving (8.44 g/100g × 450 g serving = 37.98 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 72 × 37.98 / 100 = 27.3 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266.67 mg per 100 g, yielding approximately 1200 mg sodium per standard 450 g bowl — approaching 52% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single serving.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce when ordering, limit portion to 200–250 g, avoid adding table salt or soy sauce, and account for this dish's sodium contribution within the total daily sodium budget.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice porridge base carries a high glycemic index (~72) and a glycemic load of approximately 27 per standard 450 g bowl, which can produce a significant postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 200–250 g to reduce glycemic load, pair the dish with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response, and avoid sweet condiments; turmeric present in the Nghệ An preparation has minor evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 0.89 g per 100 g; eel provides omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for cardiovascular health; however, sodium at 266.67 mg per 100 g requires portion control.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is broadly suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease due to its low saturated fat and omega-3 content from eel, but sodium intake should be managed by requesting reduced fish sauce and limiting portion size to 200–250 g per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Eel omega-3 fatty acids, turmeric curcumin, and ginger gingerols provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit; saturated fat is very low at 0.89 g per 100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This dish is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients; those with nightshade sensitivity should request omission of chilli, and fish sauce should be reduced to keep sodium well below 400 mg per 100 g.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cháo lươn Nghệ An (Nghe An Eel Porridge) is a traditional Vietnamese rice porridge dish originating from Nghệ An province in north-central Vietnam. It is prepared by slow-simmering white rice into a smooth, thick congee base, then adding tender pieces of freshwater eel (lươn) that have been stir-fried or braised with turmeric, ginger, garlic, shallots, and chilli — a flavour profile distinctive to the Nghệ An regional style. The dish is finished with fish sauce for seasoning and garnished with spring onion, fresh herbs, and crispy shallots. The texture is silky and comforting, with a savoury, mildly spiced broth carrying the earthy sweetness of eel and the warm, golden colour of turmeric. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 93 kcal, 6.7 g protein, 3.3 g fat, and 8.4 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-protein, low-fat food. Sodium is elevated at 267 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce, and a standard 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium. The rice-dominant matrix gives the dish a high glycemic index (~72) and a glycemic load of ~27 per bowl, which is clinically relevant for type 2 diabetes management. Eel contributes omega-3 fatty acids and the anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger add functional value, particularly for cardiovascular and joint health. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast or light meal in Vietnamese households and street-food settings.
common_user_queries: What is cháo lươn Nghệ An?
How many calories in Vietnamese eel porridge?

What makes Nghệ An eel porridge different from regular eel porridge?
Is Vietnamese eel porridge high GI?
What are the ingredients in cháo lươn Nghệ An?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Cháo lươn Nghệ An as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighted-ingredient modelling using USDA SR Legacy data for freshwater eel and plain rice porridge, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. The input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7; values were retained after Atwater cross-check confirmed plausibility. GI is an analogous estimate from rice congee data in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this composite Vietnamese eel porridge dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as of 2026-04-16; eel omega-3 content is qualitatively noted but not quantified per 100 g of the composite dish. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish not available in approved sources; estimated ~200 mg/100 g qualitatively from eel and herb components but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered as a numeric value. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite traditional Vietnamese preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables at the composite dish level as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace for this preparation; no industrial partially hydrogenated oils are used. country_of_origin in input was listed as Singapore, which appears to be a data entry error; corrected to Vietnam (Nghệ An province) based on the dish's well-established Vietnamese regional provenance.
